data.title: Holocaust Museum: Walz's Anne Frank comparison 'deeply offensive'
data.description:
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) is facing criticism from the U.S. Holocaust Museum after he compared Anne Frank hiding from the Nazis in Germany to the current experience of children in Minnesota amid Operation Metro Surge.

“Anne Frank was targeted and murdered solely because she was Jewish. Leaders making false equivalencies to her experience for political purposes is never acceptable,” the Washington, D.C.-based museum wrote on the social platform X.

“Despite tensions in Minneapolis, exploiting the Holocaust is deeply offensive, especially as antisemitism surges,” the post continued. It was shared on the eve of International Holocaust Remembrance Day, which commemorates the anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz-Birkenau.

The museum’s rebuke came a day after Walz commented during a press conference on the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol agent, suggesting children were “hiding in their houses” because of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raids, urging President Trump to withdraw federal law enforcement from the state.

“We have got children hiding in their houses, afraid to go outside,” Walz said. “Many of us grew up reading that story of Anne Frank. Somebody’s gonna write that children’s story about Minnesota.”

The comparison to Frank, who died in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in 1945 at the age of 15, quickly sparked backlash online.  

Shabbos Kestenbaum, a Jewish-American activist who sued Harvard University in early 2024 over their alleged failure to protect students against antisemitism, called the Democratic governor “evil” in a post online.

“One million Jewish children were killed during the Holocaust. Illegal immigrants are offered thousands of dollars to take a free flight home,” Kestenbaum wrote.

It was not the first time Walz attempted to draw similarities between the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and Nazi Germany.

During a commencement speech in May of 2025, Walz likened ICE agents to a “modern-day Gestapo,” referencing the brutal secret police force that enforced Nazi law and helped carry out the Holocaust.

“They’re in unmarked vans, wearing masks, being shipped off to foreign torture dungeons, no chance to mount a defense, not even a chance to kiss a loved one goodbye, just grabbed up by masked agents, shoved into those vans, and disappeared,” he said at the time.

Celebrities, including author Stephen King and musician Bruce Springsteen, have also drawn parallels between the two.

“The United States, the ideals and the values for which it stood for the past 250 years, is being tested as it has never been in modern times,” Springsteen said during a recent concert in New Jersey, slamming federal law enforcement for using “Gestapo tactics.”

Scholars have previously argued that using the Holocaust as an analogy in any political or ideological sense can be dangerous because it oversimplifies a complex historical situation.  

“Careless Holocaust analogies may demonize, demean, and intimidate their targets. But there is a cost for all of us because they distract from the real issues challenging our society, because they shut down productive, thoughtful discourse,” Edna Friedberg, a historian at the Holocaust Museum, wrote in 2018.

“At a time when our country needs dialogue more than ever, it is especially dangerous to exploit the memory of the Holocaust as a rhetorical cudgel,” Friedberg continued. “We owe the survivors more than that. And we owe ourselves more than that.”

data.title: DHS stresses ICE will be under Italian authority at Olympics
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said on Tuesday that U.S. immigration officers will remain under the purview of Italian officials at the upcoming winter Olympic games. 

Officers with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), which is a branch of DHS, will assist with security operations at Italy’s Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games. The competitions begin on Feb. 6 and run through Feb. 22. 

The department responded to the news on the social platform X on Tuesday. 

“Obviously, ICE does not conduct immigration enforcement operations in foreign countries,” reads a post on DHS’s account. “At the Olympics, ICE’s Homeland Security Investigations is supporting the U.S. Department of State’s Diplomatic Security Service and host nation to vet and mitigate risks from transnational criminal organizations. All security operations remain under Italian authority.”

ICE’s involvement in the games has been met with criticism from some Italian officials, who cite U.S. law enforcement’s role in killing two people in Minnesota over the last month. Milan’s mayor told local media that ICE is “not welcome in Milan, without a doubt.”

Giuseppe Conte, Italy’s former prime minister and longtime critic of right-wing Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, condemned “street violence and killings” at the hands of U.S. immigration agents, saying, “we cannot allow” ICE at the Olympics. 

“We cannot allow this,” Conte wrote in an X post translated from Italian. “After our protests, our Government had tried to downplay it, but these latest statements speak clearly about ICE’s determination to come and ensure ‘security’ in Italy as well. 

“Enough with the bows and scrapes—let’s set our own boundaries too,” he continued. “The Meloni Government must make clear decisions.”

Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi has said he doesn’t “see what the problem would be” if the U.S. officers helped to secure the games, The Associated Press reported. Piantedosi is reportedly meeting with the U.S. ambassador to Italy on Tuesday to talk about ICE’s role at the games.

data.title: Who are the 'domestic terrorists' in Minneapolis? Certainly not Pretti and Good.
data.description:
The events of 9/11 changed the nation and the world in numerous ways. Airport security was transformed after a group of 19 men hijacked four airplanes and used them as weapons to destroy the Twin Towers in New York City and inflict damage to the Pentagon. It led to the creation of the Department of Homeland Security, whose mission is “to secure the nation from the many threats we face.”  

Sept. 11, 2001, made the terms “terrorist” and “terrorism” part of everyday speech.

Today, the term “domestic terrorism” is being widely used by the Trump administration. His presidential memorandum, “Countering Domestic Terrorism and Organized Political Violence,” describes “domestic terrorists [as] wag[ing] a violent assault against democratic institutions, constitutional rights, and fundamental American liberties.” 

Domestic terrorism is defined by the Federal Bureau of Investigation as “violent, criminal acts committed by individuals and/or groups to further ideological goals stemming from domestic influences, such as those of a political, religious, social, racial, or environmental nature.” 

In Minneapolis, Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers are patrolling the area in search of “dangerous criminal illegal aliens … including murderers, rapists, gang members, and perpetrators of fraud.” In its Jan. 19 press release, Homeland Security highlighted 13 such individuals, with descriptions of their crimes. The department also noted that during the six weeks of operations to date, it had arrested “3,000 criminal illegal aliens including vicious murderers, rapists, child pedophiles, and incredibly dangerous individuals.” A complete list of such individuals and their crimes has not been released. 

So how much longer will such operations continue if all illegal aliens are to be arrested? To estimate this number, there are around 390,000 immigrants in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area (as of 2023) and around 164,000 non-citizens who may be at risk of deportation.

Given that Homeland Security claims to be searching for criminals in this group, and that around 8 percent of the adult population commits felonies, that means that around 13,000 people are their target population. However, studies have found that the felony rate among undocumented immigrants is around one-half the rate for U.S.-born citizens, bringing this number down even further.

At their current pace, these operations in Minneapolis should be completed within 10 weeks. If some of the non-citizens were in the process of becoming legalized, the time frame could be even shorter. 

However, the price paid by the residents of Minneapolis to date includes the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti at the hands Homeland Security officers. Both were shot as legal protesters of immigration enforcement — one by an ICE officer, one by a Border Patrol agent. Protest is something the U.S. Constitution gives them the legal right to do. 

The secretary of Homeland Security immediately labelled Good as a domestic terrorist and Pretti’s actions as acts of domestic terrorism, simply because they opposed how immigration law was being enforced in their community. Their interactions with immigration officers and agents were captured by eye witnesses and on multiple videos, providing a clear picture of their interactions and what transpired.   

Given the president’s statement on domestic terrorism on Sept. 25, any groups or individuals that wage an assault on constitutional rights are domestic terrorists. By this definition, when federal officers attack or kill citizens engaged in constitutionally protected public protests, are the feds acting as domestic terrorists? Partisan spin is only creating greater divides and making the situation worse. 

What is most disturbing about these immigration enforcement operations is not what they are doing, but how they are going about doing it. Secretary Kristi Noem’s assertions that the operations are needed due to Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey not protecting their own people are symptomatic of an operation smeared in politics. If the operation is truly necessary, why even mention the governor? Moreover, how are these operations protecting U.S. citizens in Minneapolis if they are killing U.S. citizens in Minneapolis?

Minneapolis did not experience the current social unrest until the Department of Homeland Security began using these tactics in the city. If a foreign terrorist group were to enter the city and created the same level of disruption, the FBI, local police and the Minnesota National Guard would be called up to bring stability and calm to the area. If foreign terrorists were to kill local people, investigations would be undertaken and criminal charges would be issued.

What immigration officers are doing, even with their mission supported by the administration, is no different than what a foreign terrorist operation would execute. 

It is time to refocus operations in Minneapolis for their intended purpose — not as a gestapo-like police force of the administration. 

Sheldon H. Jacobson, Ph.D., is a computer science professor in the Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. As a data scientist, he uses his expertise in risk-based analytics to address issues impacting public policy.

data.title: Biden:  Minnesotans 'have suffered enough at the hands of this Administration'
data.description:
Former President Biden on Tuesday praised Minnesotans protesting federal immigration enforcement operations in the wake of two recent shootings.

“What has unfolded in Minneapolis this past month betrays our most basic values as Americans,” Biden wrote in a statement on the social platform X. “We are not a nation that guns down our citizens in the street. We are not a nation that allows our citizens to be brutalized for exercising their constitutional rights. We are not a nation that tramples the 4th Amendment and tolerates our neighbors being terrorized.”

He praised Minnesotans for helping each other “in unimaginable circumstances, speaking out against injustice when they see it, and holding our government accountable to the people.”

“Minnesotans have reminded us all what it is to be American, and they have suffered enough at the hands of this Administration,” Biden continued. “Violence and terror have no place in the United States of America, especially when it’s our own government targeting American citizens. No single person can destroy what America stands for and believes in, not even a President, if we — all of America — stand up and speak out. We know who we are.”

The former president called on “full, fair, and transparent investigations into the deaths of” Renee Good and Alex Pretti. An Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer shot and killed Good earlier this month after administration officials claimed she attempted to hit the officer with her SUV, a claim that caused uproar and fueled protests. Pretti died after a fatal shooting on Saturday involving U.S. Border Patrol officers. Authorities said he was killed for approaching officers with a handgun, but video footage shows a phone in Pretti’s hand.

“Jill and I are sending strength to the families and communities who love Alex Pretti and Renee Good as we all mourn their senseless deaths,” Biden said, referring to former first lady Jill Biden.

Biden’s call to Minnesotans comes days after former President Obama and former first lady Michelle Obama called Pretti’s death “a wake-up call to every American, regardless of party, that many of our core values as a nation are increasingly under assault.”

Democratic and some Republican lawmakers have called for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s death. Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) warned on X that the credibility of ICE and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) “are at stake.”

“Any administration official who rushes to judgment and tries to shut down an investigation before it begins are doing an incredible disservice to the nation and to President Trump’s legacy,” Sen. Thom Tillis (R-S.C.), an outspoken critic of the president, said on X.

On Monday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt that DHS and the FBI are investigating the shooting, while U.S. Customs and Border Protection are investigating the incident as part of an internal review.

data.title: Greenland minister: Trump push to acquire bases as sovereign US territory a 'red line'
data.description:
A senior Greenlandic official dismissed President Trump’s desire for the U.S. to gain sovereignty over the land on which its military base sits. 

Naaja Nathanielsen, Greenland’s minister of industry, raw materials, mining, energy, law enforcement and equality, told USA Today on Sunday that the president’s proposal is a “red line.”

Trump claimed to the New York Post Friday that the U.S. will secure control over the land occupied by Pituffik Space Base, where the military conducts missile defense and space surveillance operations.

“We’ll have everything we want,” the president added. “We have some interesting talks going on.”

But Nathanielsen, echoing remarks from Greenlandic Prime Minister Jens-Frederik Nielsen and Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen, said Sunday that her country “giving up sovereignty is not on the table for now.”

Last week, Trump reached the “framework” of a deal on Greenland, which he has been interested in acquiring throughout his second term, with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte. The president also backed off imposing 10 percent tariffs on various European nations over their support for Greenland’s sovereignty. 

The agreement, which has not been finalized, gives the U.S. preferential access to the Danish territory’s minerals and updates the 1951 deal between the U.S and Greenland. 

That agreement allowed the U.S. military to operate in the country’s designated defense areas.

“The Government of the United States of America may enjoy, for its public vessels and aircraft and its armed forces and vehicles, the right of free access to and movement between the defense areas through Greenland, including territorial waters, by land, air and sea,” the agreement read. 

“This right shall include freedom from compulsory pilotage and from light or harbor dues. United States aircraft may fly over and land in any territory in Greenland, including the territorial waters thereof, without restriction except as mutually agreed upon.”

data.title: Hannity: ICE raids at Home Depot not 'good idea'
data.description:
Fox News host Sean Hannity said Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officials should not be conducting raids at Home Depot or other businesses.

“Now, is ICE perfect? They’re not. Can they do a better job? They can,” he said during an episode of “The Sean Hannity Show” on Monday. “Do I think that, you know, some of the optics at times, do I think that going into Home Depots and arresting people there is a good idea? I don’t.”

“I don’t think the people that — if you’re working at a place, you’re not the immediate problem. Eventually, we’ll have to deal with those people that didn’t respect our laws, borders and sovereignty,” he added.

Hannity’s criticism follows the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti, who was killed by a Border Patrol agent while protesting immigration enforcement in Minnesota. 

Trump administration officials said Pretti was carrying a 9mm handgun, but bystander footage shows him helping a woman up from the ground after she was pepper sprayed, moments before the shooting. 

White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller alleged Pretti was a “domestic terrorist” before details of the shooting fully emerged sparking public outrage.

“There has got to be a program where people get to come forward,” Hannity said on Monday. “I think they should then be given transportation home, they can get a check for what, $2,000, and they can apply to come back in the country legally.”

“We can vet them, we can do a health check on them and make sure they won’t be a financial burden on the American people. I think that’d be a more responsible, reasonable way to deal with that problem,” the news host added.

MS Now’s Joe Scarborough seemingly agreed with Hannity, suggesting different policies be set in place for law abiding migrants working in the U.S.

“I am struck by Sean Hannity’s words and struck because that position has been what I’ve been saying all along, what I’ve been saying since 1994. And I’m struck that the administration didn’t start here … most Americans believe that if you are here illegally that you should be deported, as Sean Hannity said, people have been talking about this for years, give people money to get back home,” Scarborough said Tuesday in an episode of “Morning Joe.”

“Then have those people reapply to come back to America and yes, get in line with people all over the world that are applying and trying to do this the correct way,” he continued. “But if you’ve been in America for a long time, if you’ve been law abiding, if you’re an asylum seeker, certainly if you’ve had children that have served in the military, you’re at the front of the line.”

Both Scarborough and former White House Correspondents Association President and Bloomberg reporter Jeff Mason said the administration’s immigration policies are “definitely struggling” to resonate with Americans.

In addition to Pretti’s death, Mason highlighted the killing of Renee Good, who was shot by an ICE officer earlier this month while attempting to flee the scene during a traffic stop. 

“Number one, if you’ve lost Sean Hannity, then you’re definitely struggling at the White House under this policy. Number two, I think it’s important to note that the tone shift is also a reflection of the fact that the tone is set by the top and set at the top,” Mason told Scarborough on Tuesday.

“Stephen Miller is a very, very important policymaker in this White House,” the reporter said. ” President Trump and Vice President Vance, look at the responses that they made to the killing of Renee Good.”

“That was said at the top and then amplified by people like Secretary Noem and, of course, Stephen Miller. The fact that Tom Homan is going in now, he is still a very, very big Trump acolyte,” he added. “He’s got some differences with a few people in the administration, but he is absolutely somebody who is an implementer of President Trump’s policies.”

Mason continued, “But the political pressure that is now coming from Republicans, not to mention Democrats, and I would add to that the polling that has shown that the electorate is losing confidence in President Trump’s immigration policies, that is having an impact, and that is leading to this shift.”

data.title: Doomsday Clock moves closer to zero-hour
data.description:
The world slipped further toward the abyss over the past year, with the Bulletin of Atomic Scientists on Tuesday moving the hands of the famed Doomsday Clock four seconds closer to midnight, at 85 seconds. 

The 80-year tradition of the Doomsday Clock is meant to serve as an urgent call to action for populations to demand change and governments to reverse factors moving the world closer to catastrophe.

Those factors include nuclear annihilation as well as climactic disasters, artificial intelligence advancing faster than the ability to govern it; biological risks and spread of disease; and the rise of autocracies and proliferation of mis- and dis-information. 

“We’re now jumping four seconds, which is a large jump, to 85 seconds, which is the closest it’s been ever,” said Daniel Holz, chair of the Science and Security Board of the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists and a physics professor with the University of Chicago.

“The main message is we should be worried, but there are many things that can be done that would turn back the clock,” he continued.

“This is a fundamentally optimistic exercise. The whole point of this is there are ways to turn back the clock. We need to band together and take those actions. It’s in all of our interest to do so.”

The Doomsday clock has moved forward over the past year – it froze at 90 seconds to midnight over the course of 2023 – and while President Trump’s return to office is not described as the driving factor, the scientists say he is taking actions that worsen the present challenges. 

“We are living through an information Armageddon,” said Maria Ressa, investigative journalist and CEO of the news-site Rappler who won the 2021 Nobel Peace Prize for her work challenging authoritarian rule of the former Philippines President Rodrigo Duterte.

“Information integrity knowing the difference between fact and fiction. That is the mother of all battles.”

Top of the list is the upcoming expiration of the nuclear arms control treaty between the U.S. and Russia, called NEW Start and that ends in February. The treaty allows information sharing and limits on U.S. and Russia nuclear stockpiles.

“I don’t want to make it sound like this is simple, but this is a piece of low hanging fruit that the Trump administration should have seized months ago,” said Jon Wolfsthal, director of global risk at the Federation of American Scientists (FAS) and who served as special assistant to the president for national security affairs during the Obama administration. 

He said Trump and Putin “could agree tomorrow” to extend the limits under NEW Start, and blamed Trump for failing to task any official with prioritizing the issue. 

“The reality is, under multiple administrations before Trump, this was the daily work of government and international security. And under Donald Trump it has ceased. There are no smart people dedicated to protecting the United States, working this problem, engaged with our adversaries to try to reduce this danger, and that’s what has to change.”

data.title: DC Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton announces retirement days after terminating campaign
data.description:
Washington, D.C. Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D), 88, announced on Tuesday that she’s retiring after her current term in Congress, just days after her campaign filed a termination report without explanation.

“The privilege of public service is inseparable from the responsibility to recognize when it’s time to lift up the next generation of leaders,” Norton said in a statement posted on X. “For D.C., that time has come.”

“With pride in all we have accomplished together, with the deepest gratitude to the people of D.C., and with the great confidence in the next generation, I announced today that I will retire at the end of this term,” she added.

Norton’s campaign filed a termination report on Sunday, signaling the D.C. delegate would not be seeking reelection for another term. However, her campaign offered no comment or explanation about its termination report for several days, raising questions about whether the congresswoman even knew she was ending her own campaign.

Norton faced mounting pressure to resign and a handful of primary challengers. The D.C. delegate previously said at different times that she would be running for reelection, only for her team to later walk back her remarks.

The dynamic became even more awkward when a number of elected officials issued statements celebrating the congresswoman after her campaign filed her termination report while Norton or her staff initially offered no comment or announcement on her candidacy.

Norton is among a growing number of lawmakers who have announced their retirement as the Democratic Party has faced calls for generational change and new candidates, with some arguing that some of the party’s older incumbents are not meeting the moment for the party during President Trump’s second term.

data.title: Families of two men killed in Caribbean boat strikes sue US government
data.description:
Family members of two Trinidadian men killed in a U.S. airstrike last October have sued the government, alleging wrongful death and extrajudicial killing amid the Trump administration’s campaign of boat strikes off the coast of Venezuela.

It’s the first federal lawsuit filed over the military’s attacks on alleged drug-trafficking vessels in the Caribbean Sea, which have killed more than 100 people since September.

That includes Chad Joseph, 26, and Rishi Samaroo, 41, whose relatives say were killed in the October 14 strike that President Trump described as a “lethal kinetic strike on a vessel affiliated with a Designated Terrorist Organization (DTO).”

Joseph’s mother, Lenore Burnley, and Samaroo’s sister, Sallycar Korasingh, brought the lawsuit on their behalf. They’re represented by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), which in a statement called the boat strikes “heinous acts of people who claim they can abuse their power with impunity around the world.”

“In seeking justice for the senseless killing of their loved ones, our clients are bravely demanding accountability for their devastating losses and standing up against the administration’s assault on the rule of law,” said ACLU senior counsel Brett Max Kaufman. 

The relatives contend in their lawsuit that the Oct. 14 strike is part of an “unprecedented and manifestly unlawful” military campaign of lethal strikes against small boats in the Caribbean and eastern Pacific Ocean, alleging 36 armed attacks have been launched against boats in international waters since Sept. 2 and some 125 people have been killed.

They wrote in a 23-page complaint that the government acted “unilaterally,” without congressional authorization, and that officials including Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth have “boasted” about the killings by posting videos of the strikes online.

“These premeditated and intentional killings lack any plausible legal justification,” the complaint reads. “Thus, they were simply murders, ordered by individuals at the highest levels of government and obeyed by military officers in the chain of command.”

The Trump administration has justified the strikes by pointing to the flow of illicit drugs into the U.S., brushing off the need to ask Congress for a declaration of war.

The Hill has requested comment from the White House, and the Pentagon declined to comment, citing the ongoing litigation.

However, the relatives said Joseph traveled to Venezuela to fish and for farm work and Samaroo worked on a farm in Venezuela where he cared for goats and made cheese.

The relatives brought their claims under two federal statutes: the Death on the High Seas Act and the Alien Tort Statute. The former lets family members sue for wrongful deaths that occur at sea, while the latter allows foreign citizens to bring lawsuits in U.S. federal courts for human rights violations.

Baher Azmy, legal director of the Center for Constitutional Rights, which also represents the challengers, called the strikes “lawless killings in cold blood.”

“Killings for sport and killings for theater, which is why we need a court of law to proclaim what is true and constrain what is lawless,” Azmy said.

—Updated at 11:33 a.m. EST

data.title: Have we kissed the Fourth Amendment goodbye?
data.description:
The Democratic midterm campaign ads will write themselves. 

Images of multiple masked government agents, decked out in tactical gear, weapons drawn, restraining an individual or “perp walking” someone half-naked from a house in the snow. They will show protesters, who look like average Americans, shouting and filming. These pictures are creating very negative voter perceptions and raising serious questions, even if the Trump administration did everything right.

If voters were appalled at images of immigrants flooding the southern border, they are just as appalled, maybe more so, at ongoing images of rough-and-tumble encounters with federal immigration enforcement.

The current focus is on the Minneapolis area, but news stories and protest marches are occurring in many other parts of the country.

When a confrontation occurs and social media erupts, the Trump administration and ICE officials immediately claim, before any investigation, that federal agents were completely justified in whatever actions they took. And they engage in character assassination of the individual involved in the altercation.

Many bystanders and local officials have taken the opposite view. The videos that emerge don’t always support the administration’s claims. It’s very difficult for average citizens to know who’s right — and that’s a real problem for President Trump.

Even worse, now we learn the Department of Homeland Security has decided to ignore the Fourth Amendment’s requirement that law enforcement have probable cause of a crime and a warrant signed by a judge before entering someone’s home. The agency is using “administrative warrants,” signed by an official within the agency, bypassing the protections afforded by the Fourth Amendment. That development is allowing ICE agents to burst into people’s homes, in some cases by mistake.

The Trump administration is defending this practice. Jimmy Percival, general counsel for the Department of Homeland Security, recently wrote in the Wall Street Journal that illegal immigrants do not have the same rights as citizens. “Under federal immigration law, officers may issue an administrative warrant, which means that the probable-cause finding is made by an executive-branch officer rather than a judicial officer.”

Percival continues, “The American people gave President Trump a mandate to restore law and order, in part by removing criminal illegal aliens from the country.” Not everyone will agree that what’s occurring in Minneapolis is a restoration of law and order. What Percival doesn’t say is that the shift to administrative warrants is to speed up the process to meet Trump’s deportation demands. It’s the speed, not the accuracy, that matters to Trump.

It may be true that illegal immigrants do not have the same rights as citizens, but they do have rights and there is a long and contentious debate over what those rights are. 

But the problem for Trump is the perception rather than the facts. Even if ICE agents were justified in their actions, and in some cases they weren’t, continual images of multiple masked agents with weapons drawn forcing an individual to the ground or dragging someone away — or even shooting them — is a political gift to Democrats.

While Trump has gotten strong reviews for addressing the problem at the border, he’s getting panned for his deportation tactics. A new poll conducted by the New York Times and Siena Research Institute found that 63 percent of registered voters disapprove of how ICE is handling its job. Only 34 percent approve.

Republicans have been more supportive of the ICE operations than Democrats or Independents. But Republicans might be more skeptical if they remembered one of conservatism’s key concerns: Government can abuse its power.

Republicans and conservatives historically have been very supportive of law enforcement, although they have become critical of the FBI for abusing its power. Law enforcement can and sometimes does use its power in unlawful or inappropriate ways. That’s why the U.S. Constitution — especially the Third through the Eighth and the 14th Amendments — and state and local laws seek to impose requirements, limits and oversight on law enforcement actions.

Voters might give the benefit of the doubt to an administration that exemplifies self-restraint and errs on the side of safety when using its power. But that’s not the Trump administration. Trump has made it very clear he intends to challenge any effort to limit what he can do on a whole range of issues — militarily, economically, politically and judicially.

Given Trump’s affinity for doing what he wants whenever he wants, relying on administrative warrants that depend on an “executive-branch officer,” who may be fired for not toeing the administration’s line, should raise significant questions among all voters, including conservatives. Conservatives vigorously defend the First and Second Amendments, regardless of who is in the White House. They should defend the other amendments, including the Fourth Amendment, just as vigorously. 

As Democrats begin to campaign for the midterm elections, their campaign ads will continually remind voters which party has lost sight of our Fourth Amendment safeguards.

Merrill Matthews is the Texas state chair of Our Republican Legacy.
